era_context: The year of the French Revolution's outbreak. The ancien régime is collapsing under the weight of financial crisis, famine, and Enlightenment ideology. The Estates-General convenes in May; the Third Estate breaks away to form the National Assembly in June; the Bastille is stormed on 14 July; feudalism is abolished in August; the Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen is adopted in September. Across Europe, monarchies watch with alarm. In North America, the U.S. Constitution has just been ratified and George Washington is inaugurated as the first president. The Industrial Revolution is accelerating in Britain.

events.title: U.S. Constitution ratified and George Washington inaugurated
events.description: The U.S. Constitution, drafted at the Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia in 1787, was ratified by the required nine states by June 1788. On 30 April 1789, George Washington was inaugurated as the first President of the United States in New York City (the temporary capital). The Constitution established a federal system with separation of powers among executive, legislative, and judicial branches. Washington's inauguration marked the beginning of the federal government's operation under the Constitution. The new government immediately faced the task of establishing precedents, creating executive departments, and managing the nation's finances. Washington's leadership and the Constitution's framework would shape American political development for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The Constitution's ratification and Washington's inauguration are precisely documented with unambiguous dates.

events.title: Nootka Sound Incident: Spanish seizure of British fur trading posts
events.description: In 1789, Spanish forces seized British fur trading posts at Nootka Sound on Vancouver Island, capturing British ships and traders. The seizure was part of Spain's attempt to enforce its monopoly on the Pacific coast north of California. The incident provoked a major diplomatic crisis between Spain and Britain, bringing the two powers to the brink of war. Britain mobilised its navy and demanded Spanish withdrawal. The crisis was eventually resolved by the Nootka Sound Convention (1790), which granted both powers equal rights to trade and settle in the region. The incident demonstrated Britain's growing naval power and willingness to challenge Spanish imperial claims.
key_figures: William Pitt the Younger (British Prime Minister)
events.certainty_note: The seizure is documented in Spanish and British sources. The date and sequence of events are well-established.

events.title: Storming of the Bastille and outbreak of the French Revolution
events.description: On 14 July 1789, Parisian crowds stormed the Bastille, a medieval fortress and state prison in eastern Paris. The storming was triggered by fears that the king was preparing to use troops to suppress the National Assembly (which had broken away from the Estates-General on 17 June). The Bastille held only seven prisoners at the time, but it was a symbol of royal tyranny and arbitrary power. The storming resulted in the death of the fortress's governor, the Marquis de Launay, and about 100 attackers. The event marked the symbolic beginning of the French Revolution and the end of the ancien régime. The storming of the Bastille became the defining moment of the Revolution and is celebrated as France's national holiday.
key_figures: Marquis de Launay (Governor of the Bastille)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by eyewitnesses and participants

events.certainty_note: The storming of the Bastille is extensively documented by contemporary witnesses, official records, and newspapers. The date and sequence of events are uncontested.
cross_references: 1789-005 (Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen)

events.title: Abolition of feudalism in France
events.description: On the night of 4 August 1789, the National Assembly voted to abolish feudalism in France. In a dramatic session, deputies from the nobility and clergy renounced their feudal privileges, and the Assembly formally decreed the end of feudal tenure, seigneurial dues, and the privileges of the nobility and clergy. The abolition was confirmed by subsequent decrees in August and September. This measure dismantled the legal and economic foundations of the ancien régime and represented a revolutionary transformation of French society. However, the abolition was not absolute: some feudal dues were converted into monetary payments, and full compensation for lost privileges was promised but never fully delivered. The abolition of feudalism was one of the Revolution's most significant achievements and set the stage for the creation of a society based on legal equality.
key_figures: Vicomte de Noailles (first to renounce feudal privileges)
events.certainty_note: The abolition of feudalism is precisely documented in National Assembly records. The date and sequence of decrees are well-established.

events.title: Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen adopted
events.description: On 26 August 1789, the National Assembly adopted the Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen, a foundational document of the French Revolution. The Declaration asserted universal principles of human rights, including liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression. It proclaimed the sovereignty of the people and the principle of legal equality. The Declaration was influenced by Enlightenment philosophy and the American Declaration of Independence (1776). It became the preamble to the French Constitution of 1791 and has remained a cornerstone of French constitutional law and international human rights law. The Declaration's principles—though not always honoured in practice—represented a revolutionary break with the ancien régime and established the ideological foundation for modern democracy and human rights.

sources.name: Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen (primary text)

events.certainty_note: The Declaration is a formal document with precise adoption date. Its text and significance are uncontested.

events.title: Estates-General convenes and Third Estate breaks away
events.description: On 5 May 1789, the Estates-General convened at Versailles. This was an assembly of representatives from the three estates (clergy, nobility, and commoners) called by King Louis XVI to address the financial crisis. The Third Estate (commoners), which represented 98% of the population but had limited power in the Estates-General, demanded equal representation and voting by head rather than by estate. When the king refused, the Third Estate broke away on 17 June 1789 and formed the National Assembly, swearing the Tennis Court Oath to continue meeting until a constitution was drafted. This act of defiance marked the beginning of the Revolution and the end of the ancien régime's political structure.
events.certainty_note: The convening of the Estates-General and the formation of the National Assembly are precisely documented. Dates and sequence of events are uncontested.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Battle of Focşani
events.description: On 1 August 1789, Russian and Austrian forces defeated Ottoman forces at the Battle of Focşani in Wallachia. The battle was part of the Russo-Turkish War (1787–1792) and the Austro-Turkish War (1788–1791). The Russian victory, achieved under General Aleksandr Suvorov, demonstrated Russian military superiority and contributed to the Ottoman Empire's decline. The battle resulted in significant Ottoman casualties and the loss of territory in the Balkans. The victory strengthened Russia's negotiating position and contributed to the eventual Russian gains in the Treaty of Jassy (1792).

sources.name: Russian military records and Suvorov's correspondence

events.certainty_note: The battle is documented in Russian, Ottoman, and Austrian sources. The date and outcome are well-established.

events.title: Haitian Revolution begins: slave uprisings in Saint-Domingue
events.description: In 1789, enslaved people in Saint-Domingue (the wealthiest colony in the Caribbean) began organised uprisings against their enslavers. The uprisings were triggered by the news of the French Revolution and the Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen, which inspired hopes for freedom among the enslaved population. The uprisings were also driven by the brutal conditions of slavery and the colony's internal conflicts between white colonists, free people of colour, and enslaved people. By the end of 1789, several plantations had been destroyed and hundreds of enslaved people had joined the rebellion. These early uprisings would escalate into the Haitian Revolution, which would ultimately result in the abolition of slavery and the establishment of Haiti as an independent Black republic in 1804.
key_figures: Enslaved people of Saint-Domingue (unnamed leaders of early uprisings)

sources.name: Later Haitian historiography and oral traditions

events.certainty_note: The beginning of slave uprisings in 1789 is attested in colonial records, but specific dates and details of individual uprisings are often unclear. The year 1789 is assigned by convention as the beginning of the broader revolutionary process.

events.title: Tipu Sultan's military campaigns in southern India
events.description: Tipu Sultan, the ruler of Mysore, continued his military campaigns against neighbouring states and the British East India Company. In 1789, Tipu launched the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War (1789–1799), attacking British-allied territories and seeking to expand Mysore's power in southern India. Tipu's forces were initially successful, but the British, under the command of Lord Cornwallis, mobilised superior resources and eventually forced Tipu into a defensive position. The war would last a decade and ultimately result in Tipu's defeat and death in 1799. Tipu's military campaigns were driven by his ambition to modernise Mysore and resist British expansion, but they ultimately failed to prevent British dominance in southern India.

sources.name: British East India Company records and military reports

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War in 1789 is documented, but specific dates and details of individual campaigns vary between sources.

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Macartney Embassy planned and prepared
events.description: In 1789, the British government began planning the Macartney Embassy to China, a major diplomatic initiative aimed at establishing formal relations with the Qing Dynasty and opening Chinese ports to British trade. The embassy was conceived as a response to Britain's growing commercial interests in Asia and the need to establish diplomatic channels with China. Lord George Macartney was selected as the ambassador, and preparations began for the journey. The embassy would depart in 1792 and arrive in China in 1793. The planning of the embassy reflected Britain's growing confidence in its naval and commercial power and its ambitions to reshape the global trading system in its favour.
key_figures: William Pitt the Younger (British Prime Minister)
events.certainty_note: The planning of the embassy is documented in British government records, but the exact date of its conception varies slightly in sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The storming of the Bastille is sometimes portrayed as a spontaneous popular uprising, but modern scholarship emphasises the role of organised political factions and the deliberate spread of rumours about royal troop movements. The abolition of feudalism on the night of 4 August is sometimes presented as a complete and immediate end to feudal relations, but historians note that the abolition was partial and that feudal dues were often converted into monetary payments rather than eliminated. The Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen is sometimes cited as establishing universal human rights, but it explicitly excluded women and enslaved people from its protections, and its principles were not consistently applied in practice. The Haitian Revolution is sometimes portrayed as beginning in 1791 with the Bois Caïman ceremony, but slave uprisings began earlier in 1789, suggesting a longer and more complex revolutionary process.
historiographic_note: The year 1789 is exceptionally well-documented for France, with rich archival sources, contemporary accounts, and extensive historiography. The U.S. Constitution and Washington's inauguration are also well-documented. Documentation of events in Asia, Africa, and the Ottoman Empire is thinner and often filtered through European sources. The Haitian Revolution is documented primarily through colonial records; enslaved people's own voices are largely absent from contemporary written records. The year reflects the biases of surviving archives: government records, parliamentary proceedings, and published narratives dominate, while popular movements and non-elite perspectives are underrepresented. The French Revolution's events are so extensively documented that they have generated enormous historiographical debate, with competing interpretations of causation, significance, and consequences.

graph_edges.note: The storming of the Bastille emboldened the National Assembly to abolish feudalism.
The Third Estate's formation of the National Assembly provoked the king's military response, which triggered the storming of the Bastille.
The Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen provided the ideological foundation for Jewish emancipation in 1791.
The 1789 slave uprisings escalated into the coordinated rebellion of 1791.
The U.S. Constitution's ratification and Washington's inauguration set the stage for the Bill of Rights in 1791.
